relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Job answers Zophar.
He asks them to truly listen, and let listening be their comfort.
He asks for space to speak before they mock again.
His complaint is not merely against humans, so impatience is understandable.
He tells them to turn, be appalled, and cover their mouths.
The memory of what he sees makes him tremble.
Why do the wicked live, age, and become powerful?
Their children are established before their eyes.
Their houses are peaceful, without dread.
Their herds are fertile.
Their children dance like a flock.
Music and joy fill their days.
They spend their days in good and descend suddenly to Sheol.
Yet they tell the Divine to depart.
They ask what profit there is in serving Shaddai.
Job rejects their counsel, even while admitting their prosperity.
He asks how often the wicked’s lamp is really put out.
Are they truly swept away like chaff?
If harm is stored for children, Job says the guilty one himself should know it.
Let his own eyes see the consequence.
What good is delayed punishment after his months are cut off?
No one can teach the Divine knowledge.
One person dies strong, at ease, and secure.
His body is full of vitality.
Another dies bitter, never tasting good.
Both lie down together in dust.
Job knows the friends’ schemes against him.
They ask where the wicked house has gone.
Job tells them to ask travelers who know the road.
The evil person can be spared in calamity.
Who confronts him to his face?
He may even be honored at burial.
Humanity follows the same path in countless number.
So their comfort is empty, and their answers betray the truth.
